title: The Best Photo
summary: A brief look at devices selecting a top photo in a stack.
showNotes: Part of the Supernova podcast series where Master of Media Design students investigate algorithms embedded in our everyday lives.
transcript:
Hi folks, welcome to Supernova, where we discuss algorithms beyond us. I'm your host, Peter Ha, and today I'll briefly talk about mobile photography: how we select and manage our photos in a world where capturing moments is as easy as tapping a screen.

With our mobile devices always at our fingertips, it has never been simpler to take a photo. Many of us snap multiple shots to ensure we get the best angle, the best pose, the best memory. But this raises an interesting question: how do we decide what's worth keeping, and what really defines the "best" photo?

Fortunately, tech companies have stepped in to solve this dilemma for us. For example, Take Tinder's AI Photo Selector. This feature scans your camera roll to identify your most flattering portraits. Or look at Apple's built-in photo app. When you use Burst mode to take a rapid series of photos, the device automatically groups them together and selects the best shot for you. Similarly, the Live Photo feature captures a moving image and then chooses the most optimal frame as the main photo.

Android users aren't left behind, either. In November 2023, Google introduced a feature called Photo Stack. This groups similar images together, and in your photo feed, you'll see a small square icon with a number, indicating how many images are in that stack. It's yet another way technology is helping us manage our ever-growing photo libraries.

Curious about how these "best" photos are selected, I asked a few people for their thoughts.

Can you just tell me a little bit about how you go about taking a photo? And if you do take multiple photos that look similar, how do you select the one that you want to keep?

Mai Ha:

"So for the photos, it gives you like the best of all the shots. • And then sometimes it says, oh like a do you want to look at other shots? And then like I go through the scrolls and see there's anything that's a little bit better for maybe like facial expressions."

Mashqurur Rahman:

"I don't know the parameters of good photo I think I'm a person who likes symmetry. So I think if the photo looks symmetrical from different angles, that is something I really like. Sometimes I'll be like oh, on pictures or maybe in terms of like colors, like, like, good color, whatever pictures I'm taking of or like the arrange, like I like photos and items of like, of course and arrangement that looks interesting to me. Sometimes I would take that or take out place myself in there to take about all that.

That's my criteria, but looking at other people's photos, obviously, that's a very different thing, because different people have different angles."

How do you think the phone selects the best photo then?

MH:

"That is a good question. I think they actually look for facial features too and like how clear it is as well. With some prints, my view of like what's a good facial feature might not be what the phone thinks. So that's why I like to go through it just in case there's like a better like body position or facial expression."

MR:

"I have no idea."

Take some guesses, no right or wrong answers.

MR:

"Maybe how the lighting is, how the composition might be. Obviously there are pretty set criteria. Oh probably, obviously whether it's blurred or not."

Some of their ideas aligned with a concept called image triage. In the paper Cosaliency: Where People Look When Comparing Images by Jacobs, Goldman, and Schechtman, the researchers found that people tend to select photos based on factors like image noise, focus, motion blur, exposure, human pose, facial expression, and object orientation.

But this isn't a new problem. Even before smartphones became ubiquitous, managing digital photographs was a growing challenge. Back in 2003, researchers Rodden and Wood explored this in their paper How Do People Manage Their Digital Photographs?. They observed that transitioning from physical to digital photography doubled the size of people's photo collections. They also noted that while digital photos allow us to capture more good moments, they also generate far more bad ones. Their research foresaw the need for systems to group and filter photos automatically—a need we see fulfilled today.

To conclude, because we no longer have to worry about the cost of film, we've gained the freedom to capture not just special moments but also the ordinary and mundane. Thanks to advancements in technology, even those everyday moments can be transformed into the "best" versions of themselves—curated by algorithms designed to make our lives easier. But this raises an important question: when technology takes on the role of deciding what's meaningful, what does that say about how we value our own memories? Are these curated photos truly the ones we'd choose ourselves? Or are they reflections of what an algorithm thinks we want to see?

Thank you for tuning in to Supernova. I'm Peter Ha, and I'll catch you in the next episode, where we'll explore more algorithms shaping our world.
